root_cause.text: The vulnerability is caused by the use of `SpelView` to render OAuth approval and error templates, which unsafely evaluates user-controllable input (such as authorization scopes or error messages) as Spring Expression Language (SpEL) expressions, leading to Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) and potential Remote Code Execution (RCE).
root_cause.evidence: The patch removes the `SpelView` class entirely, which contained `parser.parseExpression(name)` and `expression.getValue(context)`. It also adds a test case `testApprovalPageWithSuspectScope` that explicitly verifies that a malicious SpEL payload (`${T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec(\"cd ..\")}`) passed as a scope is safely HTML-escaped instead of being evaluated.

agent_logs.root_cause_candidate: The vulnerability is caused by the use of `SpelView` to render OAuth approval and error templates, which unsafely evaluates user-controllable input (such as authorization scopes or error messages) as Spring Expression Language (SpEL) expressions, leading to Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) and potential Remote Code Execution (RCE).
agent_logs.evidence_points: The patch removes the `SpelView` class entirely, which contained `parser.parseExpression(name)` and `expression.getValue(context)`. It also adds a test case `testApprovalPageWithSuspectScope` that explicitly verifies that a malicious SpEL payload (`${T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec(\"cd ..\")}`) passed as a scope is safely HTML-escaped instead of being evaluated.

intent_record.intent_summary: Remove the SpelView class and its template rendering functionality.
intent_record.what: The SpelView class and its associated file have been completely deleted.
intent_record.how: Removes the SpelView template rendering functionality from the application, meaning any code relying on it will no longer compile or function.

intent_record.intent_summary: Update import statements to accommodate new dependencies in the approval page controller.
intent_record.what: Updated and reordered import statements to include new Spring Security, Web, and Servlet classes.
intent_record.how: Provides the necessary class references for the controller to utilize new types such as CsrfToken and AuthorizationRequest.

intent_record.intent_summary: Fix security vulnerabilities (XSS and SpEL injection) by manually building the HTML approval page with proper escaping instead of using SpEL view evaluation.
intent_record.what: Replaced the SpelView implementation with a custom View object and refactored HTML generation to use a StringBuilder instead of String replacement on static templates. Also added HTML escaping to dynamic content like clientId, CSRF parameters, and scopes.
intent_record.how: By avoiding SpEL evaluation for the approval view template and escaping HTML inputs using HtmlUtils.htmlEscape, this change prevents Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and potentially Remote Code Execution (RCE) via malicious input evaluated by SpEL.

intent_record.intent_summary: Update imports to include View and HttpServletResponse.
intent_record.what: Reordered existing imports and added new imports for View and HttpServletResponse.
intent_record.how: Makes the View and HttpServletResponse classes available for use within the file.

intent_record.intent_summary: Change interpolation token to mitigate template injection vulnerabilities.
intent_record.what: Changed the interpolation syntax in the HTML error template string from ${errorSummary} to %errorSummary%.
intent_record.how: Prevents expression language engines from automatically evaluating the token, thereby stopping potential template injection.

intent_record.intent_summary: Replace SpelView with a standard View to prevent potential SpEL injection vulnerabilities in the whitelabel error endpoint.
intent_record.what: Replaced SpelView with an anonymous View implementation that uses simple string replacement.
intent_record.how: Prevents the evaluation of the error message as a Spring Expression Language (SpEL) expression, writing the HTML content directly to the response instead.
Removes the test suite for SpelView, meaning these tests will no longer execute during the build.

intent_record.intent_summary: Remove unused imports to clean up the code.
intent_record.what: Removed several unused import statements from the Java test file.
intent_record.how: Cleans up the file namespace and compilation context without affecting any runtime behavior.

intent_record.intent_summary: Add necessary imports for collections and assertions.
intent_record.what: Added imports for java.util collections and org.junit.Assert.assertTrue.
intent_record.how: Makes the Collections, HashMap, Map, and assertTrue classes available for use in the current Java file.

intent_record.intent_summary: Add tests for Whitelabel Approval endpoint to verify scope escaping and HTML generation.
intent_record.what: Added three new unit tests to WhitelabelApprovalEndpointTests: testApprovalPageWithSuspectScope, testApprovalPageWithScopesInForm, and testApprovalPageWithoutScopesInForm.
intent_record.how: Verifies that the approval page correctly escapes potentially malicious scopes to prevent injection attacks and generates the expected HTML layout with or without scopes.

intent_record.intent_summary: Update HTML attribute assertions to use double quotes instead of single quotes to fix failing tests.
intent_record.what: Changed single quotes to double quotes within string literals used in assertions checking HTML content.
intent_record.how: Updates the test assertions to match the newly generated HTML format which uses double quotes for attributes, thereby allowing the tests to pass.

intent_record.intent_summary: Update HTML string assertions to use double quotes instead of single quotes.
intent_record.what: Single quotes were replaced with double quotes in the HTML string assertions.
intent_record.how: Updates the test to correctly match the updated HTML output formatting.

intent_record.intent_summary: Fix failing tests by updating HTML assertions to expect double quotes for attributes instead of single quotes.
intent_record.what: Updated HTML string assertions to use escaped double quotes instead of single quotes.
intent_record.how: Aligns the test assertions with the updated HTML output generation, ensuring the authorization page content is correctly validated.

intent_record.intent_summary: Update test assertions to expect double quotes in HTML attributes.
intent_record.what: Changed single quotes to escaped double quotes in HTML string assertions within the verifyAuthorizationPage test method.
intent_record.how: Updates the test assertions to match the updated HTML rendering output which now uses double quotes for attributes instead of single quotes.

grouping_result.intent: Remove the SpelView class and its template rendering functionality.
Update imports to include View and HttpServletResponse.
Change interpolation token to mitigate template injection vulnerabilities.
Replace SpelView with a standard View to prevent potential SpEL injection vulnerabilities in the whitelabel error endpoint.
Remove unused imports to clean up the code.
Add necessary imports for collections and assertions.
Add tests for Whitelabel Approval endpoint to verify scope escaping and HTML generation.
Update test assertions to expect double quotes in HTML attributes.
Update HTML string assertions to use double quotes instead of single quotes.

consistency_review.core_intent: Update import statements to include new dependencies such as View and HttpServletResponse.
Mitigate template injection and XSS vulnerabilities in the application views.
Update test assertions to expect double quotes instead of single quotes for HTML attributes to fix failing tests.

relevance_review.reason: The group removes the SpelView class, which is explicitly identified as the root cause of the SSTI vulnerability.
The group consists only of updates to import statements, which are peripheral changes and do not directly address the core vulnerability logic.
The group directly addresses the vulnerability by replacing the unsafe SpEL view evaluation with manually built HTML and proper escaping to prevent template injection.
The group directly replaces the vulnerable SpelView with a standard View, directly addressing the root cause of the SpEL injection vulnerability in the error templates.
Removing the SpelViewTests file is directly related to the vulnerability root cause, as it reflects the removal or deprecation of the vulnerable SpelView class that caused the SSTI.
Removing unused imports is a general code cleanup task and does not directly address or relate to the root cause of the Server-Side Template Injection vulnerability.
Adding imports for collections and assertions is a purely structural or test-scaffolding change and does not directly address the SpEL injection vulnerability.

The group adds tests for the Whitelabel Approval endpoint to verify that authorization scopes are properly escaped, which directly tests the fix for the SSTI vulnerability in the OAuth approval templates.

The group only updates test assertions to match formatting changes (double quotes instead of single quotes) in the HTML output, which does not address the core template injection vulnerability itself.
The changes only update quote styles in HTML string assertions within tests, which is a minor stylistic/test maintenance change and does not address the SSTI vulnerability root cause.

candidates.rationale: This line returns a ModelAndView that uses the vulnerable SpelView class to render the approval endpoint template, leading to SSTI when user-controlled scopes are evaluated.
This line returns a ModelAndView that uses the vulnerable SpelView class to render the error endpoint template, leading to SSTI when user-controlled error messages are evaluated.
This is the line inside the SpelView class where the actual SpEL expression (which can contain malicious user input) is unsafely evaluated, resulting in Remote Code Execution.

vuln_statements.explanation: This statement uses the vulnerable SpelView to render the approval endpoint template, passing user-controlled model attributes that are unsafely evaluated as SpEL expressions.
This statement uses the vulnerable SpelView to render the error endpoint template, which evaluates user-controlled error details as SpEL expressions.
This is the actual location where the SpEL expression is evaluated, directly leading to the execution of the injected malicious code.

Add support for ApprovalStore in @EnableAuthorizationServer

If user defines a @Bean of type ApprovalStore the Authoriza
bic_trace_step.code_snippet:
commit 787c9b47d801b0cf08d35bca4805986c43f56836
Author: Dave Syer <dsyer@gopivotal.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 17 16:14:03 2014 +0000

    Add support for ApprovalStore in @EnableAuthorizationServer
    
    If user defines a @Bean of type ApprovalStore the AuthorizationEndpoint and
    WhitelabelApprovalEndpoint change their behaviour accordingly.

diff --git a/spring-security-oauth2/src/main/java/org/springframework/security/oauth2/provider/endpoint/WhitelabelApprovalEndpoint.java b/spring-security-o
bic_trace_step.reason: The code uses `SpelView` to render the template in `getAccessConfirmation` (`new SpelView(template)`). The `template` string is constructed by concatenating user-provided scope keys directly into the template via `createScopes` (`SCOPE.replace("%scope%", scope).replace("%key%", scope)`), which are then evaluated as SpEL expressions. This implements the vulnerable pattern described in the root cause, leading to SSTI.

The `SpelView` evaluates placeholders as SpEL expressions via `parser.parseExpression(name).getValue(context)`, leading to the Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) vulnerability.

diff --git a/spring-security-oauth2/src/main/java/org/springframework/security/oauth2/provider/endpoint/SpelView.java b/spring-security-oauth2/src/main/jav
bic_trace_step.reason: The commit introduces SpelView, which uses a PropertyPlaceholderHelper to find '${...}' placeholders and evaluates their contents as SpEL expressions using 'parser.parseExpression(name)' and 'expression.getValue(context)'. This allows evaluation of user-controllable input as SpEL, which is the root cause of the vulnerability. There are no restrictions or safe contexts applied.
